Case title: Citation-grounded multi-paper synthesis for social-policy literatures
Short description: Use retrieval-augmented synthesis to generate evidence maps or literature-survey memos from a fixed social-policy corpus with citation checking.
Empirical unit of analysis: Literature-survey memos generated from fixed corpora plus citation logs
Candidate roles tested: Protocol and provenance architect; corpus pluralist; accountability and labor allocator
Key typology question: Does provenance design matter more than fluent summary quality once synthesis is automated?
Observable artifacts / traces: Citation logs, retrieval snippets, unsupported-claim checks, human correction records
Key outcomes observed: Citation accuracy, unsupported claims, source coverage, correction burden
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: Grounded and non-grounded systems perform equally well on claim support and coverage
Main confounds / risks of misreading: OpenScholar is not social-science-specific, so transfer to policy literatures must be verified
Why this case has high leverage: It pressures provenance architecture and accountability rather than simple summarization.
collector_notes: Generated from workbook and workbook-linked sources.
